caveats: a: The BLS occupation table covers all NAICS 483 water transportation rather than 483114 and therefore mixes cargo, inland, and passenger operations.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and customer-service employment may be concentrated in larger passenger operators.
a: The estimate excludes tasks already automated through online ticketing, kiosks, and standard dispatch software.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation for LMM coastal passenger firms.
rho: Peak-season exceptions and safety incidents may require more human staffing than ordinary-day pilots imply.
rho: The estimate applies only to the opportunity counted in a, not to total labor.
e: The provided LMM firm count is modeled from receipts and a margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA or a target list.
e: The ferry census does not map exactly to NAICS 483114 and its ownership evidence is from 2015 operations.
e: NAICS is assigned at establishment level while eligibility is a firm-level control-transfer concept.
s5: Neither source isolates passenger transportation, maritime firms, LMM EBITDA, or completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The Census statistic uses 2018 data and responding employer-business owners only.
s5: An intended exit can become a closure or internal succession and therefore not qualify.
q: The funding and fare-regulation evidence is from 2015 and includes inland and public ferry operations outside the frozen lens.
q: The source measures revenue structure, not contractual treatment of AI savings.
q: Retention will vary sharply between private ticket routes, regulated utilities, and cost-reimbursed public concessions.
d5: NCFO explicitly cautions against comparing census years because respondent populations and imputation differ.
d5: FTA capacity-equivalent service miles measure supplied service, not passenger demand, and emphasize transit reporters.
d5: The national range masks severe divergence between commuter, island, vehicle, and tourism-dependent routes.
o: The evidence establishes current legal and safety requirements rather than a measured future quantity share.
o: Some functions may migrate to remote operations centers while an accountable operator remains.
o: Modal substitution and route closure are assigned to d5, not counted again here.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators earn passenger and vehicle ticket revenue, charter revenue, and public or private contract payments; routes range from market-priced private services to regulated-fare or subsidized public-service concessions, with the operator supplying vessels, licensed crews, safety compliance, reservations, terminals or terminal interfaces, and accountable transport.
lens.excluded_activities: Foreign-port deep-sea passenger transport, inland passenger transport outside the Great Lakes System, scenic round-trip sightseeing, dinner and excursion cruises, floating casinos, public authorities that are not transferable firms, captive internal fleets, dormant vessel shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat place-to-place passenger ferry, coastal passenger, Great Lakes passenger, and crewed passenger-vessel charter services to travelers, employers, institutions, or public agencies.

caveats: The BLS occupation table covers all NAICS 483 water transportation rather than 483114 and therefore mixes cargo, inland, and passenger operations.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and customer-service employment may be concentrated in larger passenger operators.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated through online ticketing, kiosks, and standard dispatch software.
a.change_evidence: A firm-level payroll and time study across ticketing, contact center, dispatch, crew scheduling, terminal operations, finance, safety reporting, maintenance, and onboard roles, combined with logs of work already automated by reservation systems, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Water transportation employs 18.84% of workers in office and administrative support, including an unusually visible customer-service population, ticket agents, dispatchers, bookkeepers, and general clerks. Passenger operators add reservation changes, disruption messaging, refund handling, accessibility inquiries, rostering, incident documentation, and marketing content that AI can assist. Wage weighting and already-digital ticketing reduce exposure, while captains, engineers, deck crews, terminal safety, loading, maintenance, and emergency response remain physical and safety-critical.

d5.bridge: BTS ferry counts and FTA service metrics cover ferry operations beyond NAICS 483114 and do not directly measure constant-price, constant-quality demand. The 2024 NCFO reports 105.8 million passengers from 165 responding operators, while FTA shows ferryboat capacity-equivalent vehicle revenue miles rising from 60 million in 2019 to 64 million in 2024. I use near-restored and expanding service supply plus diverse commute, island-access, and tourism uses to center five-year demand near current levels. Service supply can overstate demand when subsidies preserve routes, and NCFO totals cannot be trended cleanly because respondent populations change.
caveats: NCFO explicitly cautions against comparing census years because respondent populations and imputation differ.
FTA capacity-equivalent service miles measure supplied service, not passenger demand, and emphasize transit reporters.
The national range masks severe divergence between commuter, island, vehicle, and tourism-dependent routes.
d5.change_evidence: Route-level 2019-to-2026 ridership, passenger-miles, vehicle counts, real fares, service miles, subsidy commitments, employer return-to-office patterns, tourism bookings, and five-year plans from FTA NTD and NCFO operators would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Passenger demand is route-specific: island lifeline and vehicle crossings are durable, while commuter routes remain exposed to telework and tourism routes to discretionary spending. Recent service supply is above its 2019 level, but ridership counts and census coverage are uneven. Population growth, congestion, and new routes provide upside; weak fare recovery, remote work, severe weather, and substitute bridges or transit provide downside.

e.bridge: The National Census of Ferry Operators covers fixed-route ferry operations across coastal and inland waters and includes public agencies, rather than the provided private-firm LMM population for NAICS 483114. Its 2015 data show both private and public operators and multiple vessel-ownership arrangements. I use that structure to discount the modeled LMM count for public or captive operations, shells, nonrecurring charters, nontransferable concessions, and weakly compliant fleets; including inland operators may overstate the diversity of small private targets but the SUSB-based n should already exclude government entities.
caveats: The provided LMM firm count is modeled from receipts and a margin bridge rather than observed EBITDA or a target list.
The ferry census does not map exactly to NAICS 483114 and its ownership evidence is from 2015 operations.
NAICS is assigned at establishment level while eligibility is a firm-level control-transfer concept.
e.change_evidence: A named roster of the 36 modeled LMM firms with NAICS verification, private versus public status, recurring ridership or contract revenue, vessel ownership, route and terminal rights, certificates of inspection, customer concentration, and transferable management would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly fits external passenger transportation, and most active private firms should sell repeat tickets or contracted service. Eligibility falls where the operating right is tied to a public authority, route permit, terminal access, vessel certificate, or personal license that cannot transfer cleanly, or where a nominal firm lacks a standalone crew and management organization.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 483114 ($455,135,521) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 483114 ($975,920,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $341,470 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 483114); class <5: 131 firms, avg revenue/firm $349,489 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,355 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $964,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $94,769 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 15 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,292,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,208,323 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 14 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,087,643 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,089,915 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $29,841,714 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,933,441 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $123,034,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,094,242 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 36

o.bridge: Coast Guard credential rules and GAO's review apply broadly to US passenger and commercial vessels rather than measuring the year-five operator-required share for LMM 483114 firms. Coast Guard identifies credentialed masters and certificates of inspection for small ferries carrying more than six passengers, while GAO finds commercial autonomous use narrow and US law premised on onboard crews. I translate these current legal and safety requirements into a high operator-required share; the proxy could overstate durability if remote-operation approvals or statutory crew changes arrive faster than expected.
caveats: The evidence establishes current legal and safety requirements rather than a measured future quantity share.
Some functions may migrate to remote operations centers while an accountable operator remains.
Modal substitution and route closure are assigned to d5, not counted again here.
o.change_evidence: Commercial Coast Guard approvals for uncrewed or materially reduced-crew passenger ferries, statutory changes, insurer and classification acceptance, safety performance, and awarded public concessions for remote-only operators would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Passengers may switch modes, which is reflected in d5, but a trip that remains commercial passenger transport by vessel requires an accountable carrier, certificated vessel, licensed master, trained crew, safety management, boarding control, and emergency response. AI can enhance these functions without eliminating the operating entity within five years.

q.bridge: BTS measures ferry funding and fare regulation across coastal and inland, public and private operators, not five-year retention of AI benefits among LMM 483114 firms. In 2015, 74.2% of responding operators received ticket revenue, 54.6% reported tickets supplied 75% to 100% of revenue, and 67.8% of reported route segments had regulated fares. I translate direct ticket dependence into some near-term retention but discount it for regulated fares, public-contract rebids, subsidies, competitive route pricing, and customer expectations. The older, broader census may understate modern dynamic pricing but overstate retention where regulated or subsidized service dominates.
caveats: The funding and fare-regulation evidence is from 2015 and includes inland and public ferry operations outside the frozen lens.
The source measures revenue structure, not contractual treatment of AI savings.
Retention will vary sharply between private ticket routes, regulated utilities, and cost-reimbursed public concessions.
q.change_evidence: Operator-level revenue mix, fare-setting authority, concession and service contracts, bid-cost schedules, subsidy true-ups, renewal history, and gross-margin outcomes after documented labor-saving deployments would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Direct-ticket operators can initially retain back-office savings without an explicit contractual pass-through, especially when price is set by route value and alternatives. Yet many routes have regulated fares or public funding, and government or institutional contracts can reclaim savings at renewal. Passenger competition and political scrutiny also pressure operators to share benefits through lower fare growth or better service.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation for LMM coastal passenger firms.
Peak-season exceptions and safety incidents may require more human staffing than ordinary-day pilots imply.
The estimate applies only to the opportunity counted in a, not to total labor.
rho.change_evidence: Measured deployments at several private ferry operators showing containment rates, refund and accessibility error rates, schedule-recovery performance, employee adoption, union responses, cybersecurity findings, and avoided hiring over at least two peak seasons would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: A majority of exposed reservation, customer-care, finance, rostering, and documentation tasks should be implementable within five years using human-approved workflows. Adoption is limited by seasonal demand, legacy booking and vessel systems, service-disruption edge cases, accessibility obligations, cybersecurity, union or workforce rules, and safety assurance. Current autonomous-ship use remains narrow, so implementation assumes decision support rather than removal of licensed operators.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age data and the Exit Planning Institute survey cover US employer-business owners across industries, not coastal passenger operators or completed transactions. Census reports 51% of responding owners were at least 55, while 49% of the exit-planning survey respondents wanted to exit within five years. I discount those signals heavily for delayed plans, family or management succession, closures, failed sales, nontransferable route rights, and vessel-financing complexity; self-selection among exit-planning respondents likely biases the upper signal above realized qualifying transfers.
caveats: Neither source isolates passenger transportation, maritime firms, LMM EBITDA, or completed qualifying control transfers.
The Census statistic uses 2018 data and responding employer-business owners only.
An intended exit can become a closure or internal succession and therefore not qualify.
s5.change_evidence: An owner-age and succession survey of private ferry and coastal passenger operators plus five years of closed control transactions from Coast Guard vessel-documentation changes, public concession assignments, PitchBook, GF Data, and specialist marine brokers would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Broad succession pressure supports meaningful transaction probability, but passenger-vessel safety records, terminal and route rights, fleet condition, public contracts, seasonality, and financing can make a control transfer slow or infeasible. A lower central probability than raw exit intent is therefore warranted.

principal_driver: High labor intensity and passenger-facing administrative work create implementable AI opportunity while safety rules preserve the operator role.
principal_weakness: Route-level demand, fare regulation, public funding, and transferability vary so widely that national proxies offer limited target-specific confidence.

run_meta.title: Coastal and Great Lakes Passenger Transportation

sources.supports: Defines 483114 as passenger transportation in coastal waters, the Great Lakes System, or deep seas between US domestic ports, and distinguishes inland and scenic services.
sources.title: NAICS Sector 48 Definitions: 483114 Coastal and Great Lakes Passenger Transportation

sources.supports: Reports the NAICS 483 occupation mix, including 18.84% office and administrative support employment and detailed customer-service, ticket-agent, dispatcher, bookkeeping, and clerk roles.
sources.title: Water Transportation - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Reports 2024 NCFO data from 165 of 260 known US ferry operators, including 723 vessels, 897 route segments, and 105.8 million transported passengers.
sources.title: BTS Releases Ferry New Data

sources.supports: Reports private/public operator and vessel structures, ticket and public funding shares, fare regulation, ferry use cases, vessel characteristics, scope limitations, and cautions against cross-census comparisons.
sources.title: 2016 Highlights of Ferry Operations in the United States

sources.supports: Reports ferryboat capacity-equivalent vehicle revenue miles of 60 million in 2019 and 64 million in 2024, with post-pandemic service recovery context.
sources.title: 2024 National Transit Summaries and Trends

sources.supports: Finds current commercial autonomous-ship uses are narrow and generally retain human control, with safety, cyber, legal, and statutory minimum-crewing constraints in US waters.
sources.title: Coast Guard: Autonomous Ships and Efforts to Regulate Them

sources.supports: Describes credentialed master endorsements and certificates of inspection for small passenger vessels, including ferries carrying more than six passengers in near-coastal and Great Lakes service.
sources.title: US Coast Guard National Maritime Center: Charter Boat Captain

sources.supports: Reports that 49% of 1,162 surveyed US business owners wanted to exit within five years, a cross-industry intent proxy rather than completed-transfer evidence.
sources.title: 2023 National State of Owner Readiness Report

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey using 2018 data.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

title: Coastal and Great Lakes Passenger Transportation
